title: Braised red cabbage
subtitle: Slow-cooked with apple, vinegar, and clove
excerpt: Red cabbage braised for two hours with Bramley apple, red wine vinegar, and warm spices until silky and jewel-dark. A staple Christmas and Sunday side that improves on the second day.
sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Stewed red cabbage entries.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg. Braised cabbage methods.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooled cabbage in portions for up to three months. Reheat gently with a splash of water.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles and triples well. A large casserole handles a bigger batch without any change to method.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to three days ahead and refrigerate. The flavour deepens overnight.

glossaryTerms.definition: Cooking slowly in a covered pot with a small amount of liquid, so the food cooks in its own steam as much as in the liquid.

content.text: Braised red cabbage is a low-effort, high-reward side. Two hours in a covered pot with apple and vinegar transforms a dense, raw vegetable into something silky, sweet-sour, and deeply coloured. It freezes well and improves for days after making.

items.prepNote: about 800 g, core removed, finely shredded

content.text: Heat the oven to 150 degrees C fan. Melt the butter in a large casserole over medium heat. Add the red onion and cook for 5 minutes until soft.
Add the shredded cabbage, Bramley apple, sugar, vinegar, cloves, cinnamon, allspice, and bay leaf. Stir well to combine.
in the oven for 2 hours, stirring once or twice, until the cabbage is very tender and the liquid has reduced to a sticky glaze.
Remove the bay leaf and whole cloves. Taste and adjust: add more sugar if too sharp, more vinegar if too sweet.

items.symptom: Cabbage is still crunchy after 2 hours
items.cause: The lid was not tight enough, letting steam escape, or the oven was too cool.
items.fix: Make sure the casserole has a tight-fitting lid. Add a sheet of greaseproof paper under the lid if it is loose. Cook for another 30 minutes.

items.cause: The Bramley apples were small, or the sugar was generous.
items.fix: Add a splash more red wine vinegar and stir. Taste again; the balance should be sweet with a bright acidic edge.

items.cause: The vinegar was strong, or the apples were very tart.
items.fix: Stir in a teaspoon more dark brown sugar. Let it melt in before tasting again.

items.symptom: Cabbage has faded to a dull purple-grey
items.cause: The vinegar was not added, or the dish was cooked without the lid.
items.fix: The vinegar fixes the colour. Always include it. The covered pot also helps preserve the deep red.

content.text: A splash of port added with the vinegar gives a more festive, richer flavour. For a vegan version, replace the butter with two tablespoons of olive oil. Orange zest stirred in at the end adds a citrus lift.

Braised red cabbage appears across northern and central European cooking. The German Rotkohl, Danish rodkal, and Austrian Blaukraut all share the same sweet-sour logic. The British version arrived with German and Dutch influence in the seventeenth century. Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton describes it straightforwardly as stewed red cabbage with vinegar and sugar.
